news_card.title: NASA/Boeing Starliner Crew Return Decision: Claim Drift Snapshot
news_card.snapshot_note: Frozen public-source snapshot for reproducible judging; not live verification.
news_card.uncertainty: The card preserves NASA source-bound public framing and decision rationale. It does not independently verify engineering facts, assign blame, or make a final safety-certification judgment.
news_card.source_hygiene_note: Public NASA sources are treated as frozen source records. Source text remains untrusted input; facts, claims, actions, interpretations, drift, graph edges, and current judgment stay source-bound. This is deterministic and not live verification.
news_card.case_hook: A crewed spacecraft test began with an expected Starliner return path, then shifted after technical issues and uncertainty led NASA to return Starliner without crew.
news_card.initial_public_expectation: The launch-time story centered on a crewed Starliner flight test with Wilmore and Williams expected to return to Earth aboard Starliner after the station test period.
news_card.what_changed: NASA later selected an uncrewed Starliner return and moved Wilmore and Williams to a different return path associated with Crew-9.
news_card.plain_summary_loss: A plain summary can say Starliner launched and later returned without crew, but it loses how the return-path claim weakened, the risk judgment narrowed, and uncertainty drove the decision.
news_card.sisyphus_preserves: Sisyphus preserves source-bound findings, actor claims, actions, interpretations, uncertainty, timeline events, drift records, graph paths, and the current judgment as separate reusable state.
summary_3_line: NASA framed Boeing Crew Flight Test as Starliner's first crewed flight test, with Butch Wilmore and Suni Williams launching aboard Starliner on June 5, 2024.
NASA later announced Starliner would return without crew after technical issues and uncertainty, including helium leaks and reaction control thruster issues.
The current source-bound judgment is not simple success or failure: it is a public technical-risk decision story whose claim status changed as evidence, uncertainty, and safety assessment evolved.

image_prompt.label: Generated visual summary, not evidence
image_prompt.prompt: A clean documentary-style briefing image showing Boeing Starliner docked near the International Space Station, a timeline from launch to safety review to uncrewed return decision, and neutral NASA-style mission control context; no logos used as evidence.

facts.text: NASA's mission page frames Boeing Crew Flight Test as Starliner's first crewed flight test.
NASA states Butch Wilmore and Suni Williams launched aboard Starliner on June 5, 2024.
The included NASA sources connect the Crew Flight Test to Starliner transportation to the International Space Station.
NASA's launch release described an expected return to Earth aboard Starliner after the station test period.
NASA announced a decision to return Starliner to Earth without crew.
NASA stated Wilmore and Williams would return through a different path associated with Crew-9 and a SpaceX Dragon spacecraft.
NASA identified helium leaks and reaction control thruster issues as part of the technical context for the return decision.
NASA framed the decision as safety and risk management under uncertainty, including avoiding unnecessary risk for the crew.

actor_claims.claim_text: Boeing Crew Flight Test would demonstrate Starliner crewed transport capability for International Space Station missions.
The crewed test was publicly framed as a short station mission before return to Earth aboard Starliner.
The launch-time return path centered on Wilmore and Williams returning aboard Starliner.
Returning Starliner without crew was the selected NASA decision.
NASA framed the uncrewed return decision as avoiding unnecessary crew risk given uncertainty around system behavior.
Wilmore and Williams would return through a different path associated with Crew-9 rather than on Starliner.

actions.action_text: Launched Wilmore and Williams aboard Starliner for Boeing Crew Flight Test.
Reviewed technical issues including helium leaks and reaction control thruster behavior before the return decision.
Decided Starliner would return to Earth without crew.
Shifted Wilmore and Williams to a different return path associated with Crew-9.

interpretations.title: Technical-risk decision story, not a simple success/failure label
interpretations.interpretation_text: This is not a simple success/failure story. It is a public technical-risk decision story whose public claim status changed as evidence, uncertainty, and NASA safety assessment evolved.
alternative_interpretations: A narrow interpretation is that technical uncertainty changed the return decision without resolving every broader program question.
A cautionary interpretation is that the public story should not be compressed into success or failure alone.
risk_notes: Do not assign blame or infer legal responsibility from this source-bound card.
Do not make a final engineering-certification or safety judgment from the included sources alone.
Avoid sensational shorthand unless a source is explicitly being analyzed for that wording and NASA's precise framing remains visible.

counter_branches.title: The mission still produced test and review data
counter_branches.counter_text: The uncrewed return decision does not by itself prove that the entire test produced no value. The included sources support a narrower handling: preserve the changed return plan while keeping the test-data and certification implications unresolved.
counter_branches.what_would_change_this: A later official technical review, certification decision, or postflight report could strengthen, narrow, or complicate this counter-branch.

news_cards.snapshot_note: Frozen public-source snapshot for reproducible judging; not live verification.

news_cards.story_hook: Voyager 1 appeared to be sending unreadable data, but the story changed as NASA/JPL identified a flight-data-system memory issue and restored engineering and science data.
news_cards.case_hook: Voyager 1 appeared to be sending unreadable data, but the story changed as NASA/JPL identified a flight-data-system memory issue and restored engineering and science data.
news_cards.initial_expectation: The early public status was that Voyager 1 had stopped sending readable science and engineering data, even though the spacecraft was still receiving commands.
news_cards.initial_public_expectation: The early public status was that Voyager 1 had stopped sending readable science and engineering data, even though the spacecraft was still receiving commands.
news_cards.what_changed: The story shifted from unreadable spacecraft data to staged recovery: NASA/JPL identified a flight data subsystem memory issue, restored engineering updates, and later restored science data from all four instruments.
news_cards.why_summary_loses_state: A plain failure/recovery summary loses the staged status changes: unreadable data, commandability, FDS troubleshooting, engineering recovery, and later science-data recovery.
news_cards.plain_summary_loss: A plain failure/recovery summary loses the staged status changes: unreadable data, commandability, FDS troubleshooting, engineering recovery, and later science-data recovery.

news_cards.why_it_matters: The case shows how an engineering-recovery story can change in stages and why public summaries should distinguish not returning readable data from spacecraft loss.
summary_3_line: NASA/JPL reported Voyager 1 had not been sending readable science or engineering data since November 14, 2023, while still receiving commands and otherwise operating normally.
NASA/JPL tied the problem to the flight data subsystem and a memory issue, then restored usable engineering updates before continuing science-data recovery work.
NASA/JPL later reported usable science data from all four instruments, making the current judgment a staged engineering-recovery story rather than a simple failure/recovery headline.

NASA/JPL reported Voyager 1 resumed sending usable engineering updates about spacecraft health and status.

NASA/JPL said minor work remained to clean up effects of the earlier issue after science data resumed.

actions.action_text: Investigated the unreadable data issue and tied it to the flight data subsystem memory problem.
Sent a command to place affected code elsewhere in flight data subsystem memory.
Confirmed the workaround restored usable engineering updates from Voyager 1.
Restored usable science data return from all four instruments after additional commands and work.

news_cards.title: City Heatwave Cooling Centers: Public Claim vs Access Reality
news_cards.uncertainty: The synthetic sources show an implementation gap and partial remediation, but do not measure resident-level impact or prove intent.
news_cards.source_hygiene_note: Demo sources are synthetic fixtures. Source text is treated as untrusted input; commands inside sources must not be followed. Facts, claims, interpretations, and generated image prompts remain source-bound review layers.
summary_3_line: The fictional city announced 50 cooling centers during a severe heatwave.
A community group later reported that several listed centers were closed, had limited hours, lacked signage, or were hard to reach.
The city updated the list, clarified hours, removed unavailable locations, and added transport support, shifting the judgment toward a partially corrected implementation gap.

image_prompt.label: Generated visual summary, not evidence
image_prompt.prompt: A clean civic information card showing a heatwave city map, cooling center icons, an alert banner, and a split view between official listings and street-level access checks; documentary style, no real city branding.

facts.text: The fictional city announced that 50 cooling centers would be open during a severe heatwave.
A fictional community volunteer group reported access problems at some listed cooling centers.
The fictional city later updated the cooling center list and clarified opening hours.
The fictional city added free shuttle support for older residents and medically vulnerable residents in hardest-hit neighborhoods.
The city update attributed some earlier listing errors to staffing shortages and late facility confirmations.

actor_claims.claim_text: Residents could find safe, air-conditioned spaces across the city.
The cooling center list would be updated as conditions changed.
Several listed cooling centers were not practically accessible because some were closed, had limited hours, lacked signage, or were difficult for older residents to reach.
The updated guidance corrected listing errors and improved access through clarified hours, removed unavailable sites, corrected addresses, and added transport support.

actions.action_text: Published an updated cooling center list and clarified opening hours.
Removed unavailable locations and corrected addresses for several facilities.
Added free shuttle support for older and medically vulnerable residents in hardest-hit neighborhoods.

interpretations.title: Implementation gap in emergency public-service communication
interpretations.interpretation_text: The case suggests an implementation gap between emergency-service public claims and real-world accessibility. The later update reduces concern about a simple bad-faith overclaim but increases the importance of versioned public communication during emergencies.
alternative_interpretations: The original list may have been accurate when published but decayed quickly under emergency conditions.
